reason: path-rootless 'a@b.com'; a raw @ is a legal pchar in a path segment, so no authority is involved
scheme data, path-rootless 'space', query 'test', fragment 'test'; every character is pchar
path-absolute: a single leading slash and no authority (no '//')
rootless path 'b' with an empty-but-present fragment; an empty fragment is legal
path-rootless 'example.com'; scheme colon then a rootless path, no authority
path-rootless 'example.com/path'; further '/'-joined segments are legal
repeated raw @ inside the authority; userinfo and reg-name forbid a raw @ (must be %40), so at most one @ is allowed
'//' then an empty authority/host, then path-abempty '/////bar.com/'; an empty reg-name is legal and path-abempty is *( '/' segment )
lone % in the path not followed by two hex digits (pct-encoded = '%' HEXDIG HEXDIG)
% followed by only a single hex digit in the path
% followed by non-hex digits 'zz' in the path
malformed '%2 ' triplet (space after one hex) in the reg-name; the raw space is itself illegal
valid pct-encoded %20 inside the reg-name
a raw space is not a legal URI character in a path segment
bracketed IPv6 IP-literal '[::1]' with port 8080 and path-abempty '/'
IP-literal opens with '[' but has no closing ']'
IP-literal inner 'g::1' is not a valid IPv6 address (non-hex 'g')
raw @ inside the userinfo (must be %40); two @ in the authority is a grammar failure
userinfo 'us%40er' encodes its @ as %40; the single unescaped @ is the userinfo/host separator
directly-written non-ASCII scalar 'ä' is the one tolerated extension (flagged unicode-outside-rfc3986-uri); the surrounding ASCII grammar is intact
reg-name sub-delims '!' and '$' are legal host characters
reg-name sub-delims ',' ';' '=' are legal host characters
port must be *DIGIT; 'o' is not a digit
raw '[' and ']' outside a bracketed IP-literal are not pchar in a path
IPvFuture form 'v1.fe80::1' inside the bracketed IP-literal
present-but-empty query and fragment delimiters are legal (query/fragment are *(...))
scheme then path-empty; a URI with an empty hier-part is legal
